notes: The 2022 UCC amendments introduce a new Article 12 on "controllable electronic records," with Section 12-105 defining "control" consistently with DAPL Principle 6 and Section 12-105(d) specifically being compatible with DALP Principle 7.
text_title: Amendments to the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC)
promulgating_body: Developed jointly by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute; enacted at state level by individual US jurisdictions
principle_title: UNIDROIT Principles on Digital Assets and Private Law
subtitle: Control

text_title: Amendments to the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC)

principle_title: UNIDROIT Principles on Digital Assets and Private Law
subtitle: Identification of a person in control of a digital asset

notes: The approach to “control” as provided for in Article 10 of the Digital Assets Law is based on the DAPL Principles. The Consultation paper from 4 September 2023 states: "Our approach to control aligns with Principle 6 of UNIDROIT’s Principles on Digital Assets and Private Law".
text_title: Digital Assets Law (DAL) — DIFC Law No. 2 of 2024
promulgating_body: The Ruler of Dubai
principle_title: UNIDROIT Principles on Digital Assets and Private Law
subtitle: Control

notes: Principles reflected are Principle 8 (innocent acquisition) and a limited reference to Principle 6 (control). Decree-Law No. 25 of 17 March 2023 was drafted with the DAPL Principles in mind. Article 5(4) reflects the innocent acquisition rule by protecting a good-faith acquirer who obtains an entry in the digital circulation register on the basis of a suitable title, while Article 17 refers to control of the means of access to digital financial instruments, though in a regulatory rather than private-law sense.

subtitle: Innocent aquisition

notes: The Digital Assets (Scotland) Act 2026 is consistent with several core aspects of the DAPL Principles. The Policy Memorandum cites that "a technology neutral approach to defining digital assets aligns with that adopted in cross-jurisdictional statements of relevant private law principles, notably The International Institute for the Unification of Private Law ('UNIDROIT') Principles on Digital Assets and Private Law".

notes: Ukraine's Law No. 4466-IX "On Factoring" directly implements the UNIDROIT Model Law on Factoring. The law's explanatory note states that its purpose is "implementation into Ukrainian legislation of the provisions of the UNIDROIT Model Law on Factoring, which will bring Ukrainian factoring closer to the best international counterparts".  Its core provisions are substantively modelled on the corresponding MLF articles. It contains some important deviations, such as the application to absolute assignments of receivables only.

notes: The amendments to Uzbekistan's Civil Code introduced by Law No. ZURQ-1058 were developed with the technical assistance of the EBRD, World Bank Group and International Law Institute, and are recorded by the UNIDROIT Governing Council as having been made "to align the law with the MLF". The amended provisions of Chapter 42 (Articles 749–758²) transpose key features of the MLF, and a comparison of the two texts shows  strong alignment between them.

notes: The World Bank and ILI have reported that the MLF has been used to support the UAE's Federal Decree-Law. That the UAE law was developed in parallel with, rather than after, the finalisation of the MLF may account for certain structural differences buy a comparison of the two texts confirms a high degree of similarity across their core provisions.

notes: Section One of the new Argentinian civil and commercial code deals with consent, offer, and acceptance. The wording follows the UNIDROIT Principles, which recognise both the offer-and-acceptance model and those cases in which there is a continuous process that begins with negotiations and gradually becomes more concrete.

notes: The Russian legislators’ tendency to seek inspiration in the UPICC can be clearly seen in the more recent amendments to the Civil Code that entered into force on 1 June 2015. These include the new wording of Article 434, relating to the obligation to negotiate in good faith, which is influenced by Article 2.1.15 of the UPICC and the new version of Article 393.1 incorporating the principle according to which the harm for which compensation is to be received must be of a nature that can be determined with a reasonable degree of certainty, just how it is stipulated in Article 7.4.3 of the Principles

notes: In the Report written to the President of the French Republic, the drafters of the Ordonnance explicitly highlighted the role of international standards such as the UPICC in the drafting process. In particular, Chapter III explains about Article 1118: “The second paragraph, inspired by the PDEC, the DCFR and the Unidroit principles, specifies that if the common intention of the parties cannot be determined, the meaning of the contract is interpreted according to a standard, that of the average contracting party referred to as a "reasonable person"

promulgating_body: President of the French Republic
principle_title: UNIDROIT Principles of International Commercial Contracts
subtitle: Intention of parties

notes: The Explanatory Memorandum attached to the law introducing Book 5 of the Belgian Civil Code explicitly states that “ By introducing this unilateral or non-judicial termination into the general law of contracts, the proposal follows the significant development found in the civil codes of our neighbouring countries and in soft law instruments such as Article 7.3.2 of the UNIDROIT Principles”
